one_line_takeaway: SearchAttack shows that outsourcing a jailbreak's harmful semantics to multi-hop web-search triggers plus rubric-reverse-engineered reward-chasing prompts lets search-augmented LLMs (agentic and snippet settings) synthesize higher-fidelity, higher-scope harmful content than direct-query baselines, and that this attack is markedly harder to filter with prompt-level defenses (ASR-KW only drops from 0.94-1.00 to 0.80-0.86 under combined defenses, vs. FlipAttack dropping to 0.02-0.74) or detect with LlamaGuard-3 8B (0.24 vs. 0.52 detection rate) than a conventional flip-based jailbreak.
key_finding: SearchAttack achieves a combined ASR-KW of 0.96 (averaged over AdvBench and ShadowRisk, agentic setting) vs. a 0.82 baseline average, and raises attack value from Scope/Fidelity 4.35/0.62 (baselines) to 10.20/0.94 (SearchAttack). Per Table 2, on AdvBench (ASR-KW/ASR-GPT) under agentic search SearchAttack scores 0.86/0.94 (Gemini-3-Flash), 0.94/1.00 (DeepSeek-V3.2), 0.98/0.96 (Qwen3-32B), 0.92/0.96 (GPT-4o) -- generally matching or exceeding PAIR/ReNeLLM/FlipAttack, with the largest margin on GPT-4o where FlipAttack collapses to 0.02/0.02 under agentic search. On ShadowRisk (ASR-KW/WR), SearchAttack reaches 0.99/0.75 (Gemini-3-Flash, agentic), notably higher WR than other methods, though on DeepSeek-V3.2/Qwen3-32B/GPT-4o SearchAttack's ASR-KW leads but WR is comparable to or sometimes lower than FlipAttack. Ablation (Table 3) shows removing Trigger Augmentation drops ASR-GPT most sharply (0.94/0.96 -> 0.82/0.80); removing Adversarial Auditing raises ASR-GPT (0.86->0.96) but lowers AtV Fidelity (0.92->0.87); removing Rubric Reverse Engineering lowers ASR-GPT (0.96->0.92) and Scope/Fidelity (10.2/0.87->8.9/0.89) -- vs. a Direct Query baseline of 0.02/0.00 ASR and (0.1, 0.40) AtV. Defense results (Table 4) show combined Safety Prompt + Safety Injection cuts FlipAttack's ASR-KW from 0.46 to 0.02 (Gemini) and 1.00 to 0.74 (DeepSeek), but only cuts SearchAttack from 0.94 to 0.80 (Gemini) and 1.00 to 0.86 (DeepSeek); LlamaGuard-3 8B detects FlipAttack at 0.52 but SearchAttack at only 0.24. Cross-lingual analysis: Chinese-dominant search surfaced 8,904 URLs vs. 6,461 for English-dominant search on the same queries, and weakly curated sources (Social/UGC, Other) made up 69.8% of Chinese-retrieved evidence on average, suggesting weaker moderation of non-English content by Google Search.
core_contribution: The paper proposes SearchAttack, a dual-stage red-teaming method that jailbreaks search-augmented LLMs (chatbot, snippet-search, and agentic-search settings) by (1) 'Outsourcing Injection' -- rewriting a harmful base query into a syntactic skeleton whose harmful entities are replaced by single-hop then multi-hop 'search-triggers,' generated via a multi-agent pipeline (skeleton initialization, adversarial auditing, and a sub-agent that builds a knowledge graph from live web search to create obfuscated multi-hop triggers) so the target LLM must retrieve external evidence to decode the harmful intent; and (2) 'Retrieval Curation' -- a rubric-reverse-engineering step that exploits the reward-chasing bias of RLVR-trained models by framing the harmful-content-curation task as a graded, multi-objective scoring task. The authors also build ShadowRisk, a 2,802-item (210 released) socio-temporal illicit-activity Q&A benchmark sourced from 21,670 Chinese short-video transcripts, and an Attack Value (AtV) fact-checking framework that decouples policy-bypass success (ASR) from actual factual utility of the harmful output by scoring retrieved-evidence-grounded Scope (count of verifiable actionable claims) and Fidelity (fraction of those claims verified correct).
threat_model: Black-box attacker crafting text prompts against search-augmented/agentic LLM systems (four victim models tested: Gemini-3-Flash, DeepSeek-V3.2, Qwen3-32B, GPT-4o) across three deployment settings -- no search (parametric-knowledge chatbot), one-shot snippet search, and iterative agentic search with a real search backend (Google Search API). The attacker does not need model internals or gradients; the attack payload itself is synthesized by an auxiliary LLM (DeepSeek-V3.2) driving agentic web search and a knowledge graph, and is queried once against the victim (5 attempts reported). The paper explicitly frames the risk as a 'knowledge-to-action' pathway: dual-use information already exists on the open web, and the attack's danger lies in inducing the victim LLM to retrieve, aggregate, and restructure fragmented/indirect evidence into coherent, high-fidelity operational content rather than merely surfacing already-ready-to-use harmful documents.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations/caveats: (1) ASR-GPT/WR judging is done by an LLM-judge ensemble, and even the content-based StrongReject framework was shown (Figure 4) to overlook severe factual errors in jailbreak outputs, motivating the separate AtV fact-checking layer -- but AtV itself relies on a single fact-checking agent (Claude-4.5-haiku) rather than human verification. (2) Search does not monotonically increase harm -- some models' retrieval triggers stronger refusal/citation behavior instead of synthesis, so results are model- and setting-dependent rather than uniformly worse-case. (3) ShadowRisk is grounded specifically in a 2025 China socio-temporal context and only a 210-item subset (of 2,802 curated) is released/evaluated for responsible-disclosure reasons, limiting reproducibility of the full benchmark and generalizability outside that context. (4) The attack payload synthesis itself depends on a specific auxiliary model (DeepSeek-V3.2) and specific hyperparameters (N_E=N_A=3, N_G=8, N_Q=3) whose sensitivity is not separately ablated beyond the three named components. (5) Defenses tested are limited to two prompt/context-level strategies (Safety Prompt, Safety Injection) plus one open-source guard classifier (LlamaGuard-3 8B); no retrieval-side filtering or fine-tuned defenses are evaluated, so claims of defense robustness are bounded to these particular mitigations.

datasets_used: AdvBench-50 (existing benchmark, subset per prior work)

ShadowRisk (new): 2,802 knowledge-intensive Q&A pairs curated from 21,670 videos on Chinese media platforms (e.g., Douyin), covering fraud, illegal logistics, money laundering, and cyber-enabled crime; 210-item subset released and evaluated across 8 illicit categories
quantitative_results: Combined ASR-KW (avg over AdvBench + ShadowRisk, agentic search): SearchAttack 0.96 vs. baseline average 0.82
Attack Value: Scope/Fidelity increases from baseline average 4.35/0.62 to SearchAttack 10.20/0.94

Ablation (Gemini-3-Flash, AdvBench, w/ snippet): full method 0.94/0.96 ASR, AtV (10.2, 0.87); w/o Trig. Aug. 0.82/0.80, (7.2, 0.84); w/o Adv. Audit 0.86/0.86, (9.6, 0.92); w/o Rub. Rev. 1.00/0.92, (8.9, 0.89); Direct Query baseline 0.02/0.00, (0.1, 0.40)
Defense (Table 4) ASR-KW under combined SP+SI: FlipAttack drops to 0.02 (Gemini)/0.74 (DeepSeek); SearchAttack drops only to 0.80 (Gemini)/0.86 (DeepSeek)
LlamaGuard-3 8B detection rate: FlipAttack 0.52 vs. SearchAttack 0.24

Cross-lingual URL collection: 6,461 URLs (English-dominant search) vs. 8,904 URLs (Chinese-dominant search) for the same attack queries; weakly curated sources (Social/UGC, Other) = 69.8% of Chinese-retrieved evidence on average
baselines_compared: Direct Query (no-attack baseline, ablation table only)
